title: Disconnect super cold callers: Longo
full_text: Other Editions 07 May 2026 Business Disconnect super cold callers: Longo The Australian Cliona O’Dowd 7 May 2026 484 words English AUSTLN 15 © News Pty Limited. No redistribution is permitted. The Australian Securities & Investments Commission is calling for a ban on unlicensed superannuation marketing, arguing the model has fuelled a “conveyor belt of consumer harm” that risks wiping out people’s retirement savings. In his final keynote speech as ASIC chair, Joe Longo will on Thursday point to Treasury’s current consultation on lead generators in the wake of the Shield and First Guardian fund collapses, while also raising the alarm on how AI is turbocharging online investment threats. “We have witnessed unscrupulous actors trying to exploit the significant pot of money in our superannuation system through what we suspect is industrial-scale misconduct,” Mr Longo will tell a Financial Counselling Australia conference in Cairns. “We must disrupt the lead generation model that has enabled this conveyor belt of consumer harm to occur. One way to do it, from my personal view, and something the government is consulting on, would be to ban unlicensed communications about superannuation.” Mr Longo’s comments come weeks after the Albanese government released a consultation package aimed at plugging the consumer protection gaps exposed by the Shield and First Guardian failures that cost close to 12,000 people $1bn in lost super savings. Shield and First Guardian were managed investment schemes put into liquidation over the past 18 months. In both cases, failures are alleged across the investment chain, from the research house that rated the funds, to the financial advice firms recommending them, the super trustees that put them on their platforms, and the funds themselves, which are currently under investigation. Lead generation featured heavily in the marketing of the funds to potential investors. Thousands of people were cold-called after filling in a free super health check form online, with the lead generators then passing these consumers on to advisers. Two of the super trustees caught up in the scandal, Macquarie and Netwealth, have repaid investors to the tune of $421m, while two others, Equity Trustees and ­Diversa, have refused to pay and are being sued by the corporate regulator over alleged due diligence failures. Among the reforms being considered by federal Labor are slowing down super switching, changes to compensation funding, and forcing super trustees to pay for consumer losses due to fraud or theft. A further reform proposed by the government is that unlicensed communications to consumers about superannuation would be banned and lead generation, also known as cold-calling, would be captured as a type of financial service, requiring those engaged in the activity to hold a licence. “The most effective way to address this misconduct is to stop it at the source – and a ban on unlicensed communications would certainly go a long way to achieving that,” Mr Longo said. Mr Longo will leave ASIC at the end of May, with deputy chair Sarah Court to replace him. The Australian-20260507-Australian-15-000500741208 Nationwide News Pty Ltd. summary: The Australian Securities & Investments Commission (ASIC) is advocating for a ban on unlicensed superannuation marketing, citing significant consumer harm linked to cold calling practices. This call follows the collapse of investment schemes that resulted in substantial losses for thousands of Australians. ASIC's chair, Joe Longo, emphasised the need for regulatory reforms to protect consumers from exploitation in the superannuation sector.

heading: Superannuation and Investments
key_points: ASIC is pushing for a ban on unlicensed communications regarding superannuation.
The call for reform follows the failures of Shield and First Guardian funds, which led to $1 billion in losses.
Proposed reforms include regulating lead generation and enhancing consumer protection measures.
relevance: This is relevant to actuaries and financial services professionals as it highlights ongoing regulatory changes that may impact investment practices and consumer trust in financial products.
